examples.title: Linear Search - O(n) Time Complexity
examples.description: Demonstrates linear time complexity using a simple linear search algorithm that checks each element sequentially.
examples.code:
def linear_search(arr, target):
    """
    Linear Search: Traverses the array element by element
    to find the target value.
    """
    for i in range(len(arr)):
        if arr[i] == target:
            return i  # Target found at index i
    return -1  # Target not found

# Test the function
arr = [12, 45, 67, 89, 23, 56, 78, 34, 90, 11]
target = 56
result = linear_search(arr, target)

if result != -1:
    print(f"Element {target} found at index {result}")
else:
    print(f"Element {target} not found in the array")

# Additional test: element not in array
target = 100
result = linear_search(arr, target)
print(f"Element {target} found: {result != -1}")
examples.explanation: The linear_search function iterates through each element in the array exactly once. In the worst case (element not found or at last position), it examines all n elements. The loop runs n times, making this an O(n) algorithm. Space complexity is O(1) as only a few variables are used regardless of input size.

examples.title: Bubble Sort - O(n²) Time Complexity
examples.description: Demonstrates quadratic time complexity using bubble sort, a simple comparison-based sorting algorithm with nested loops.
examples.code:
def bubble_sort(arr):
    """
    Bubble Sort: Repeatedly steps through the list,
    compares adjacent elements and swaps them if in wrong order.
    """
    n = len(arr)
    
    for i in range(n):
        # Flag to optimize: track if any swap occurred
        swapped = False
        
        # Last i elements are already sorted
        for j in range(0, n - i - 1):
            if arr[j] > arr[j + 1]:
                # Swap adjacent elements
                arr[j], arr[j + 1] = arr[j + 1], arr[j]
                swapped = True
        
        # If no swapping occurred, array is already sorted
        if not swapped:
            break
    
    return arr

# Test the function
arr = [64, 34, 25, 12, 22, 11, 90, 5, 77, 30]
print(f"Original array: {arr}")

sorted_arr = bubble_sort(arr.copy())
print(f"Sorted array:   {sorted_arr}")

# Trace through first pass
arr_test = [5, 1, 4, 2, 8]
print(f"\nTracing first pass on {arr_test}:")
for j in range(len(arr_test) - 1):
    if arr_test[j] > arr_test[j+1]:
        arr_test[j], arr_test[j+1] = arr_test[j+1], arr_test[j]
        print(f"  After swap: {arr_test}")
examples.explanation: Bubble sort uses two nested loops. The outer loop runs n times, and for each iteration, the inner loop runs (n-1), (n-2), ... times. This gives us n + (n-1) + (n-2) + ... + 1 = n(n+1)/2 = O(n²) comparisons in the worst case. Even with the optimization (early exit), worst-case remains O(n²). Space complexity is O(1) as sorting is done in-place.

examples.title: Merge Sort - O(n log n) Time Complexity
examples.description: Demonstrates divide-and-conquer approach with O(n log n) time complexity, a more efficient algorithm than quadratic sorts.
examples.code:
def merge_sort(arr):
    """
    Merge Sort: Divide and conquer algorithm that divides
    array in half, sorts each half, then merges them.
    """
    # Base case: array with 0 or 1 element is already sorted
    if len(arr) <= 1:
        return arr
    
    # Divide: find mid point
    mid = len(arr) // 2
    
    # Conquer: recursively sort both halves
    left_half = merge_sort(arr[:mid])
    right_half = merge_sort(arr[mid:])
    
    # Combine: merge sorted halves
    return merge(left_half, right_half)

def merge(left, right):
    """Merge two sorted arrays into one sorted array."""
    result = []
    i = j = 0
    
    # Compare elements from both halves
    while i < len(left) and j < len(right):
        if left[i] <= right[j]:
            result.append(left[i])
            i += 1
        else:
            result.append(right[j])
            j += 1
    
    # Add remaining elements (if any)
    result.extend(left[i:])
    result.extend(right[j:])
    
    return result

# Test the function
arr = [38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10, 55, 21, 66]
print(f"Original array: {arr}")

sorted_arr = merge_sort(arr)
print(f"Sorted array:   {sorted_arr}")

# Demonstrate the divide process
def merge_sort_with_trace(arr, depth=0):
    indent = "  " * depth
    print(f"{indent}Processing: {arr}")
    
    if len(arr) <= 1:
        return arr
    
    mid = len(arr) // 2
    print(f"{indent}Dividing into: {arr[:mid]} and {arr[mid:]}")
    
    left = merge_sort_with_trace(arr[:mid], depth + 1)
    right = merge_sort_with_trace(arr[mid:], depth + 1)
    
    result = merge(left, right)
    print(f"{indent}Merged: {result}")
    return result

print("\n--- Trace of Merge Sort ---")
merge_sort_with_trace([5, 2, 8, 1, 9])
examples.explanation: Merge sort follows the divide-and-conquer paradigm. The array is recursively divided in half log(n) times until single elements are obtained (height of recursion tree = log n). At each level (there are log n levels), we merge all n elements. Thus, time complexity is O(n log n). The space complexity is O(n) because we create temporary arrays during the merge process. Unlike bubble sort (O(n²)), merge sort scales efficiently for large datasets.
